label: State Senator
subject: Please schedule a hearing on S447, H3155, and H4013
body:
Dear [NAME],

Last year, two Greenville sisters driving a rental car were pulled over at gunpoint when a Flock Safety camera wrongly flagged their vehicle as stolen. Police drew their weapons. Both women were handcuffed. The car wasn't stolen. They are now suing Greenville PD.

This is what happens when surveillance technology runs without rules, and it's happening right here in South Carolina.

There are 155 Flock Safety cameras across Upstate SC. Flock secretly gave federal agencies direct access to local police cameras across the country, without telling those agencies. Their CEO denied it on camera in July 2025 and admitted it three weeks later.

Once that data is in the network, your local police can't pull it back. No policy change fixes that. Federal agencies don't need a warrant to look. Our own Transportation Secretary confirmed the Legislature has never addressed this.

Three bills are waiting for a hearing: S447, H3155, and H4013. All three are stalled in committee. Communities like Hays County, TX and Sedona, AZ cancelled their Flock contracts once they understood how the data sharing actually works. Even a Republican state rep from Simpsonville called for regulation years ago.

Your constituents are watching. Please push for a committee hearing.

Sincerely,
[Your name and address]

label: Greenville City Council
subject: Greenville needs a public vote on Flock Safety
body:
Dear [NAME],

Last year, two Greenville sisters driving a rental car were pulled over at gunpoint when a Flock Safety camera wrongly flagged their vehicle as stolen. Police drew their weapons. Both women were handcuffed. The car wasn't stolen. They are now suing Greenville PD.

Greenville has run 30 Flock Safety cameras since 2021, paid for with civil asset forfeiture funds, with no council vote on oversight and no rules about who can see the data, how long it's kept, or who checks how it's used. Flock secretly gave federal agencies direct access to local police cameras across the country, without telling those agencies. Their CEO denied it on camera and admitted it three weeks later.

Once Greenville's data is in that network, the city can't pull it back. No policy change fixes that. It's how the system is built. None of that requires a warrant. The city's own camera contracts are designed to pull in private HOA cameras and residents' Ring cameras, growing the network beyond what's publicly visible with no additional vote.

The council has the power to change this. I'm asking you to:
- Hold a public hearing on Greenville's Flock Safety contract and how the data is actually shared
- Pass an ordinance requiring council approval before any new surveillance technology is deployed
- Refuse to expand the system until residents have had a real say

Sedona, AZ; Hays County, TX; and Cambridge, MA all looked closely at how Flock works and decided it wasn't worth it. Greenville residents deserve the same chance to weigh in.

Sincerely,
[Your name and address]

label: Greenville County Council
subject: Greenville County needs oversight of Flock Safety cameras
body:
Dear [NAME],

The Greenville County Sheriff's Office operates 11 Flock Safety license plate reader cameras. The City of Greenville runs 57 more. All of this data feeds into a shared network where SLED retains it for three years, and Flock has given federal agencies direct access without local agencies' knowledge.

The Sheriff's Office has no county-level oversight policy governing how this data is accessed, shared, or audited. Meanwhile, the county's SafeWatch program is pulling HOA and private cameras into the same network, expanding surveillance reach without any public vote or transparency.

Flock's CEO denied federal data sharing on camera in July 2025 and admitted it three weeks later. Once location data enters the network, no local policy can pull it back. Federal agencies don't need a warrant to search it.

Last year in Greenville, two sisters were handcuffed at gunpoint after a Flock camera wrongly flagged their rental car as stolen. They are now suing. This is what happens when surveillance technology runs without oversight.

I'm asking the County Council to:
- Require the Sheriff's Office to disclose all Flock camera locations and data-sharing agreements
- Establish a county oversight policy covering data retention, access, and auditing
- Hold a public hearing before any expansion of the camera network

Hays County, TX and Sedona, AZ cancelled their Flock contracts after reviewing how the data sharing works. Greenville County residents deserve the same transparency.

Sincerely,
[Your name and address]

label: Spartanburg City Council
subject: Spartanburg needs local oversight of surveillance cameras
body:
Dear [NAME],

Flock Safety license plate reader cameras are deployed in the Spartanburg area, tracking the movements of residents with no local oversight framework. The data feeds into a national network where Flock gave federal agencies direct access without telling local police departments. Their CEO denied it publicly and admitted it weeks later.

In May 2025, Spartanburg County Sheriff Chuck Wright pled guilty to federal charges of conspiracy to commit theft from federal programs and wire fraud. The official overseeing law enforcement surveillance deployments in this area was a federal criminal. That fact alone should prompt every elected body in Spartanburg to ask hard questions about who has access to residents' location data and under what rules.

There are 155 Flock cameras across Upstate SC. No city or county in the region has passed an oversight ordinance. No one is auditing how the data is used, who sees it, or how long it's kept. Once it enters the Flock network, local agencies cannot retrieve it.

I'm asking the City Council to:
- Hold a public hearing on surveillance camera deployments within city limits
- Require disclosure of all data-sharing agreements with Flock Safety
- Pass an ordinance requiring council approval before new surveillance technology is deployed

Communities like Sedona, AZ and Cambridge, MA reviewed how Flock works and decided the risks outweighed the benefits. Spartanburg residents deserve the same opportunity.

Sincerely,
[Your name and address]

label: Spartanburg County Council
subject: County oversight is needed after Sheriff Wright's conviction
body:
Dear [NAME],

In May 2025, Spartanburg County Sheriff Chuck Wright pled guilty to federal charges of conspiracy to commit theft from federal programs and wire fraud. The county's own top law enforcement official, the person with access to Flock Safety license plate reader data tracking residents' movements, turned out to be a convicted criminal.

Flock cameras are deployed across Spartanburg County. The data feeds into a national network that Flock opened to federal agencies without telling local departments. Their CEO denied it on camera and admitted it weeks later. Once location data enters the network, no local policy can pull it back. Federal agencies don't need a warrant to search it.

The county has no oversight policy governing who accesses this data, how long it's retained, or how it's audited. Given what happened with Sheriff Wright, the absence of safeguards is not an abstraction. A convicted criminal had unsupervised access to a system that tracks where residents drive.

I'm asking the County Council to:
- Require full disclosure of all Flock camera locations and data-sharing agreements
- Establish a county oversight policy with data retention limits, access controls, and regular auditing
- Hold a public hearing before any renewal or expansion of surveillance contracts

Hays County, TX cancelled its Flock contract after learning how data sharing works. Spartanburg County residents deserve accountability, especially now.

Sincerely,
[Your name and address]

label: Anderson City Council
subject: Anderson needs transparency on surveillance cameras
body:
Dear [NAME],

Flock Safety license plate reader cameras are operating across the Upstate, including the Anderson area. There are 155 confirmed cameras in the region, and no city or county has established public oversight of how the data is collected, shared, or retained.

Flock secretly gave federal agencies direct access to local police camera networks across the country. Their CEO denied it on camera in July 2025 and admitted it weeks later. Once location data enters the Flock network, local agencies cannot pull it back. Federal agencies don't need a warrant to search it.

This isn't hypothetical. In Greenville, two sisters were handcuffed at gunpoint after a Flock camera wrongly flagged their rental car as stolen. In Spartanburg, the county sheriff who oversaw surveillance deployments pled guilty to federal criminal charges in May 2025. These are the real consequences of surveillance without accountability.

Anderson residents have a right to know how many cameras are operating in their city, who can access the data, and what rules govern its use. Right now, the answer to all of those questions is unclear.

I'm asking the City Council to:
- Disclose all Flock Safety deployments and data-sharing agreements within city limits
- Hold a public hearing on surveillance technology oversight
- Require council approval before deploying or expanding any automated surveillance system

Sedona, AZ and Hays County, TX reviewed how Flock works and cancelled their contracts. Anderson deserves the same transparency.

Sincerely,
[Your name and address]

label: Anderson County Council
subject: Anderson County needs an oversight policy for surveillance cameras
body:
Dear [NAME],

Flock Safety license plate reader cameras are deployed across the Upstate, including Anderson County. There are 155 confirmed cameras in the region, tracking where residents drive. No county in the Upstate has established an oversight policy governing how this data is collected, shared, or retained.

Flock secretly gave federal agencies direct access to local police camera networks without telling the agencies operating them. Their CEO denied it publicly and admitted it weeks later. Once location data enters the Flock network, no local policy can retrieve it. Federal agencies don't need a warrant to search it.

In Greenville, two sisters were handcuffed at gunpoint after a Flock camera wrongly flagged their rental car. In Spartanburg, the county sheriff who oversaw surveillance deployments pled guilty to federal criminal charges. These are neighboring counties, and the same technology operates here without oversight.

Anderson County residents deserve to know how many cameras track their movements, who can access that data, and what safeguards exist. Right now, no one is auditing any of it.

I'm asking the County Council to:
- Require disclosure of all Flock camera deployments and data-sharing agreements in the county
- Establish a county oversight policy with data retention limits and access controls
- Hold a public hearing before renewing or expanding any surveillance contracts

Hays County, TX and Cambridge, MA reviewed Flock's data practices and decided the risks were too great. Anderson County should do the same.

Sincerely,
[Your name and address]

label: Pickens County Council
subject: Multiple agencies run Flock cameras in Pickens County with no coordinated oversight
body:
Dear [NAME],

Multiple agencies operate Flock Safety license plate reader cameras in Pickens County, including the Pickens County Sheriff's Office and Clemson University Police. Each deployment feeds into the same national Flock network, but there is no coordinated county-level oversight governing any of them.

Flock secretly gave federal agencies direct access to local police camera networks without telling the departments involved. Their CEO denied it on camera and admitted it weeks later. Once location data enters the network, no local agency can pull it back. Federal agencies don't need a warrant to search it.

With multiple agencies operating independently, no one is tracking the total number of cameras, who can access the combined data, how long it's kept, or whether the deployments overlap. Residents moving through Pickens County may be tracked by multiple systems with no coordinated rules.

In Greenville, two sisters were handcuffed at gunpoint after a Flock camera wrongly flagged their car. In Spartanburg, the county sheriff overseeing surveillance deployments pled guilty to federal criminal charges. The Upstate has 155 Flock cameras and zero oversight policies.

I'm asking the County Council to:
- Require all agencies operating Flock cameras in Pickens County to disclose their deployments and data-sharing agreements
- Establish a unified county oversight policy covering data retention, access, and auditing
- Hold a public hearing before any expansion of the camera network

Sincerely,
[Your name and address]

label: Laurens County Council
subject: Laurens County should act on surveillance oversight before cameras expand
body:
Dear [NAME],

Across Upstate South Carolina, 155 Flock Safety license plate reader cameras track where residents drive. The South Carolina Legislature has never addressed ALPR data privacy. No county in the region has an oversight policy. And the problems are already showing up in our neighboring counties.

In Greenville, two sisters were handcuffed at gunpoint after a Flock camera wrongly flagged their rental car as stolen. In Spartanburg, the county sheriff who oversaw surveillance deployments pled guilty to federal criminal charges in May 2025. Flock secretly gave federal agencies direct access to local police camera networks without telling the departments involved. Their CEO denied it and admitted it weeks later.

Once location data enters the Flock network, no local policy can pull it back. Federal agencies don't need a warrant to search it. SLED retains the data for three years. None of this has been addressed by any level of government in South Carolina.

Laurens County has an opportunity to get ahead of this. Rather than waiting for problems to arrive, the council can establish clear rules now.

I'm asking the County Council to:
- Require disclosure of any current or planned surveillance camera deployments in the county
- Establish an oversight policy covering data retention, access controls, and auditing before any new system is adopted
- Hold a public hearing so residents can weigh in

Sincerely,
[Your name and address]

label: Local Representative
subject: Our community needs oversight of surveillance cameras
body:
Dear [NAME],

Across Upstate South Carolina, 155 Flock Safety license plate reader cameras track where residents drive. No city or county in the state has established a public oversight policy for this technology. The South Carolina Legislature has never addressed ALPR data privacy.

Flock secretly gave federal agencies direct access to local police camera networks across the country without telling the departments involved. Their CEO denied it on camera in July 2025 and admitted it weeks later. Once location data enters the Flock network, local agencies cannot retrieve it. Federal agencies don't need a warrant to search it.

This isn't hypothetical. In Greenville, two sisters were handcuffed at gunpoint after a Flock camera wrongly flagged their rental car as stolen. In Spartanburg, the county sheriff who oversaw surveillance deployments pled guilty to federal criminal charges. SLED retains license plate data for three years with no public accountability framework.

Communities across the country have reviewed how Flock works and taken action. Hays County, TX and Sedona, AZ cancelled their contracts. Cambridge, MA established strict oversight rules. South Carolina has done neither.

I'm asking you to:
- Require disclosure of any surveillance camera deployments and data-sharing agreements in our community
- Hold a public hearing on surveillance technology oversight
- Establish a policy requiring elected body approval before new automated surveillance systems are deployed

Residents deserve to know who is tracking their movements and under what rules.

Sincerely,
[Your name and address]
